# Discover the Bio-Based Materials Transforming Car Manufacturing

![](https://maycointernational.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/06/MAYCO-Blog_LinkedIn-Discover-the-Bio-Based-Materials-Transforming-Car-Manufacturing-scaled.png)

What if the future of your car was built from plants, not petroleum? As automakers race to meet global sustainability goals, the demand for eco-conscious innovation is exploding.

According to BCC Research, ***estimates show that the global value of [bio based composite materials](https://www.bccresearch.com/market-research/advanced-materials/bio-composites-market.html?srsltid=AfmBOopP4hdxLRNRl-pu_tTFVkUgHwzMNfyj8fhGNeZBFMdwrX6gI5l2) will climb from $5.9 billion in 2023 to around $9 billion by 2029.*** This signals a powerful transformation in how vehicles are designed and built. Forward-thinking manufacturers are shifting toward sustainable design as the new standard, not only a trend.

So, read on to explore how **bio based materials** are changing the game for the automotive world and what it means for your business.

## Why Bio Based Materials Matter

Car manufacturers are under growing pressure to reduce carbon emissions, waste, and environmental impact. **Bio based materials** offer a solution that aligns with global sustainability goals. Derived from renewable resources like hemp, flax, and kenaf, use these materials in place of petroleum-based plastics and synthetic composites.

***Bio-based vehicle components are now being integrated into:***

* Door panels and dashboards
* Trunk liners and insulation
* Seat backs and interior trims

These materials are lighter, which improves fuel efficiency, and they are also recyclable or biodegradable, reducing landfill waste.

## Key Examples of Sustainable Car Materials

Automakers are turning to innovative materials that support both performance and sustainability. These next examples highlight how bio based options are already making a real impact across the industry.

### Natural Fiber Composites

Flax and hemp fibers mixed with polymers provide durability and flexibility. Therefore, these composites are now used by brands like BMW and Mercedes-Benz.

### Bioplastics

Derived from corn starch or sugarcane, these plastics offer a lower carbon footprint. They’re commonly used in interior trims and seating components.

### Recycled and Reclaimed Materials

Post-consumer plastics and natural fibers can be repurposed to create an [eco-conscious car design](https://maycointernational.com/blog/the-design-process-the-art-that-shapes-quality-automotive-parts/) without sacrificing performance.

***Automakers benefit from:***

* Lower weight
* Reduced emissions
* Alignment with green mandates

## Mayco International’s Role in the Green Transition

As a tier-one automotive supplier, [Mayco International](https://maycointernational.com/blog/top-engineering-design-services-from-mayco-international/) is helping push the boundaries of eco-friendly automotive innovation. Headquartered in Michigan and operating across North America and beyond, the company partners directly with automotive OEMs like Tesla, BMW, and Chrysler to deliver cutting-edge components.

***Mayco International’s approach includes:***

* Blending post-consumer recycled content with virgin plastics
* Recycling all production scrap in-house
* Investing in lightweight, high-strength bio-materials

## Driving Toward the Future of Car Manufacturing

The evolution toward [sustainable car materials](https://www.cares-sustainableforum.com/sustainable-automotive-materials/) is more than a trend; it’s a necessity. Regulations, consumer demand, and global climate goals require automakers to reimagine their supply chains.

***Tier-one partners like Mayco are leading the charge by:***

* Offering advanced prototyping for bio based vehicle components
* Reducing energy through lean manufacturing
* Supporting OEMs in achieving ESG benchmarks

## Partner With Mayco International: Your Source for Bio Based Materials

If you’re an OEM looking to stay ahead in the green automotive movement, **bio based materials** are essential to your strategy. As a trusted automotive interior supplier, Mayco International offers unmatched expertise in engineering, tooling, and manufacturing for interior and exterior vehicle systems.

Mayco’s mission is to be the supplier of choice in delivering validated, sustainable, and complete automotive solutions.

Explore how we can support your goals with innovative, eco-conscious components. So, [contact Mayco International](https://maycointernational.com/contact/) today to learn how bio based materials can elevate your next vehicle platform.

![Bio-based foam, a new sustainable and lightweight automotive material.](/api/imaging/focalarea/33x10/414x/dam/jcr%3Ab08a959a-0404-32ef-8b6b-17a257c332ed/Bio-Based%20Foam.PNG "Bio-based foam, a new sustainable and lightweight automotive material.")

Automotive & Transportation

# Bio-based foam, a new sustainable and lightweight automotive material

Sustainability remains a top priority for automakers, despite the Environmental Protection Agency's recent proposal to scale back fuel-economy standards.

Aside from contributing to a cleaner world, the focus on sustainability is good for business. Today, a third of consumers globally choose to buy from brands they believe are doing social and environmental good, according to an international study conducted by Unilever.

Emissions reduction and lightweighting are the most common sustainability strategies. Working with IAC and BASF, Ford took its approach a step further, incorporating a bio-based foam comprised of castor oil beneath the instrument panel of its 2018 Ford Fusion.

In addition to leveraging renewable materials, BASF's [Thin-Light solution](https://plastics-rubber.basf.com/global/en/performance_polymers) provides weight savings of up to 30 percent compared to standard foam-backed instrument panels. And since lighter vehicles use less energy, the sustainable solution is doubly beneficial to the environment and was recognized as the SPE Environmental Category award-winner in 2017.

#### Bio-based foam is easily integrated into the production process

Thin-Light, part of a new generation of systems within BASF's Elastoflex E product group, addresses the challenges of developing of lighter, more intricate instrument panels.

The production process for foam-backed instrument panels involves injecting foam between the skin and support of the panel using high heat and pressure to join all elements. The new system is easily integrated into existing panel production and addresses a common challenge of adhering different materials together. BASF's Elastoflex offers superior adhesion properties to dissimilar materials.

In addition to the 30 percent weight savings it offers, the chemistry of BASF's system improves the material viscosity and composition, resulting in smaller cross sectional thicknesses and reduced foam densities. The foam can be molded to less than five millimeter cross sections, contributing to lightweighting and increased space for vehicle component packaging.

Safety is a key concern when designing the instrument panel. The foam material is required to maintain shape and form during air bag deployment, minimizing any fragmentation from impact. Although the Thin-Light system reduces the overall instrument panel size and weight, the bio-foam maintains its integrity over the life span of a vehicle.

![Bio-based foam, a new sustainable and lightweight automotive material.]( "Bio-based foam, a new sustainable and lightweight automotive material.")

#### Sustainable materials for a greener automotive industry

"When all else is equal - the benefits, the performance, the cost - we believe the customer will select a renewable resource as their material of choice," said Jeffrey Hagar, market segment manager, automotive interiors for Performance Materials at BASF.

BASF decided to use castor oil, instead of soy oil, as its renewable material source for this foam system because it has no impact on food and farming supplies. Castor oil also does not run the same risk of turning rancid and maintains superior processing physical properties.

With environmentally conscience motives driving the choice to use renewable materials, automakers can proudly promote their contributions to a greener world.

Lightweight automotive materials bring future mobility advantages

As the industry drives toward a fully autonomous future, the use of [lightweighting solutions](/global/en/automotive/learn/stories/automotive-lightweighting--3-industry-leading-innovations) becomes even more important.

For instance, a lighter weight, thinner instrument panel contributes to greater design freedom in autonomous vehicles. Instrument panels in driverless vehicles require more personalized components and technology, and automakers are challenged with integrating more equipment without adding to overall vehicle mass.

Solutions like the Thin-Light system enable weight reduction without sacrificing functionality or aesthetics.

"BASF understands the challenges that automakers are facing, whether its weight requirements, safety, emissions regulations or evolving industry trends," said Hagar. "Sustainability is a core pillar of our business. We're focused on engineering solutions that meet or exceed performance requirements but with renewable materials that contribute to environmental security."

BASF's Elastoflex product group is globally available, and BASF can adapt the technology with local support.

Highlights
----------

*   •The state-of-art of [lightweight materials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/lightweight-material "Learn more about lightweight materials from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for automobiles is reviewed. 
*   •Representative materials are introduced with potential [automotive applications](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/automotive-application "Learn more about automotive applications from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), particularly on electric vehicles. 
*   •Given the recent advances in manufacturing, modeling, and characterization, both pros and cons of materials are summarized. 
*   •Solutions for future challenges are highlighted by developing [advanced materials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/advanced-material "Learn more about advanced materials from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and/or improving the manufacturing. 

Abstract
--------

The growing challenges on fuel economy improvement and greenhouse gas emission control have become the driving force for automakers to produce lightweight automobiles. Also, the weight reduction may contribute to superior recyclability and/or vehicle performance (_e.g_., improved driving economy, braking behaviors, and crashworthiness). One effective strategy is to develop and implement lightweight yet high-performance materials as alternative solutions for conventional automotive materials such as cast iron and steel. Herein, a systematic review of available lightweight materials to produce next-generation automobiles is provided, including light alloys, high-strength steels, composites, and advanced materials in the ongoing research. By investigating the entire life cycle of automotive materials, physical/mechanical properties, characterization, manufacturing techniques, and potential applications of specific lightweight materials are discussed. Both the advantages and drawbacks of the reviewed materials are summarized, yielding the appropriate application scenarios for different lightweight materials. Given the future challenges, on expectations, the development of versatile advanced materials or improvement of the manufacturing/treatment techniques can be rather promising to resolve the possible bottlenecks and, in turn, enables more capable, safer, durable, and environmental-friendly vehicles.

1. Introduction
---------------

According to the [Corporate Average Fuel Economy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/corporate-average-fuel-economy "Learn more about Corporate Average Fuel Economy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (CAFE) standard, all [original equipment manufacturers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/original-equipment-manufacturer "Learn more about original equipment manufacturers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (OEMs) in the automotive industry are required to meet the fuel economy target by the average weight of the fleet [[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264127522006165#b0005). In terms of CO 2 emissions per kilometer drive (g/km), the average fuel economy target of passenger vehicles is also challenging for most countries and regions ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264127522006165#f0005)(a)) [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264127522006165#b0010). Particularly, the United States set the 2025 goal of average CO 2 emissions to 89 g/km, reduced by about 40% compared to that of 2015. Apart from fuel economy improvement and emission control, greater emphasis on higher performance and easier [recyclability](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/recyclability "Learn more about recyclability from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") further promote the development of lighter, stronger, and greener automobiles. Thus, it is essential to explore new materials and achieve more efficient structural design for future automobiles. So far, global OEMs have implemented a variety of effective strategies to rise to the challenges, including vigorously developing hybrid and [pure electric vehicles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/pure-electric-vehicle "Learn more about pure electric vehicles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[[3]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264127522006165#b0015), [[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264127522006165#b0020), increasing the drive train efficiency [[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264127522006165#b0025), and exploring lightweight materials for automobiles [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264127522006165#b0030), [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264127522006165#b0035), among which the top priority is placed on weight saving [[8]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264127522006165#b0040).

![Image 12](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0264127522006165-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (233KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0264127522006165-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (233KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0264127522006165-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. Pressing demand for [lightweight materials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/lightweight-material "Learn more about lightweight materials from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of automobiles. (a) Fuel economy targets in different countries (passenger vehicles) [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264127522006165#b0010); (b) Light vehicle production by the major market in million units [[9]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264127522006165#b0045).

Over the past few years, there has been a steady growth of light vehicle production in all the major markets ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264127522006165#f0005)(b)) [[9]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264127522006165#b0045). As potential substitutions for conventional [engineering materials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/engineering-material "Learn more about engineering materials from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (_e.g_., steel and cast iron), lightweight materials for automobiles can be primarily divided into four categories, light alloys (_e.g_., aluminum, magnesium, and titanium alloys), HSS family (_e.g_., conventional HSSs and AHSSs), composites (_e.g_., [carbon fiber reinforced plastics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/carbon-fiber-reinforced-plastic "Learn more about carbon fiber reinforced plastics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or CFRP), and advanced materials (_e.g_., mechanical metamaterials). Since the last century, these lightweight materials have been extensively applied to various automotive components such as dashboard, bumper, engine, body shell, wheel, suspension system, brake, steering system, [battery](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/battery-electrochemical-energy-engineering "Learn more about battery from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), seat, and gearbox ([Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264127522006165#f0010)). Typical lightweight materials and their specific [automotive applications](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/automotive-application "Learn more about automotive applications from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are summarized in [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264127522006165#t0005).

Representative [lightweight materials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/lightweight-material "Learn more about lightweight materials from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") applicable for different components of automobiles, including [light alloys](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/light-alloys "Learn more about light alloys from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (_e.g_., aluminum, magnesium, and titanium alloys), HSSs, composites (_e.g_., CFRP), and [advanced materials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/advanced-material "Learn more about advanced materials from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (e.g., metamaterials).

Generally, the decision of the applications of the new materials on automobiles is not straightforward and multi-dimensional factors need to be considered. The complete life cycle of most automotive materials covers from raw materials to the final recycling and disposal ([Fig. 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264127522006165#f0015)). The material selection and structural design can significantly depend on critical factors during each of the sub-processes, _i.e_., material fabrication (_e.g_., cost and properties), automotive component manufacturing (_e.g_., design, surface treatment, and jointing technique), vehicle integration (_e.g_., assembling), service (_e.g_., durability), and end of life/recyclability (_e.g_., scrap and emission). For instance, the OEMs may consider if the automotive components are capable of mass production based on well-established manufacturing techniques or if they can meet the goal for fuel economy target within a reasonable budget. Therefore, it is essential to take a global-perspective methodology to consider selecting appropriate lightweight materials for specific automotive components.

![Image 14](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0264127522006165-gr3.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (532KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0264127522006165-gr3_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (532KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0264127522006165-gr3.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 3. The entire life cycle of automotive materials [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264127522006165#b0075), [[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264127522006165#b0080), [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264127522006165#b0085), [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264127522006165#b0090), [[19]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264127522006165#b0095), [[20]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264127522006165#b0100), [[21]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264127522006165#b0105).

In this paper, the development and implementation of available lightweight materials in the automotive industry are comprehensively reviewed, which covers major representative light alloys, HSS families, composites, and advanced materials. A detailed overview of these competitive materials is given in terms of their remarkable performance (_e.g_., large weight reduction potential, high specific strength/stiffness, and good corrosion resistance), automotive applications, major obstacles for broader usage (_e.g_., poor formability, incompatibility with traditional manufacturing techniques, and high cost) and possible solutions. Accordingly, this review will provide considerable information about the properties (both physical and mechanical), manufacturing feasibility as well as the cost toward the promising opportunities for designing weight-saving components for automobiles. Meanwhile, the summary of ongoing research on metamaterials may point the way for future research directions. Also, the guidelines for automotive material selection and structural design may pave a new road for designing lighter, safer, and greener automobiles in the future.

2. Light alloys
---------------

### 2.1. Aluminum

First separated in 1888 as an element, aluminum possesses fascinating properties. The density of aluminum is 2.69 g/cm 3, which is approximately one-third as dense as conventional steel (7.83 g/cm 3). Accordingly, the remarkable specific behaviors (e.g., specific strength or strength to weight ratio) make it possible to manufacture lighter aluminum-based automobiles with superior performances in terms of fuel consumption, acceleration, braking, and handling [[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264127522006165#b0005), [[22]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264127522006165#b0110). On average, one ton of aluminum as a substitution for cast iron or conventional steel may reduce 18 tons of greenhouse gas emissions during the life cycle of a middle-size sedan [[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264127522006165#b0005). Meanwhile, coupling aluminum with other elements (e.g., Cu, Mn, Si, Mg, and Zn) to form alloys may endow them with more outstanding performances (e.g., enhanced [corrosion resistance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/corrosion-resistance "Learn more about corrosion resistance from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), excellent electrical and [thermal conductivity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/thermal-conductivity "Learn more about thermal conductivity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), together with higher strength compared to that of the structural steel).

From an [automotive engineering](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/automotive-engineering "Learn more about automotive engineering from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") standpoint, aluminum and its alloys have gradually moved to the forefront of lightweight materials applied to automobiles in recent years ([Fig. 4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264127522006165#f0020)(a)). In 2006, aluminum overtook cast iron as the second most-used automotive material (second to steel) in the North American market [[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264127522006165#b0005). Furthermore, the light vehicle aluminum content of the market is targeted at about 570 net pounds per vehicle for 2030, where the [cast aluminum alloy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/cast-aluminum-alloy "Learn more about cast aluminum alloy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") may contribute more than 50% ([Fig. 4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264127522006165#f0020)(b)). Specifically, an extruded aluminum bumper beam was highlighted in the crashworthiness design of the Honda Pilot for its significant role in impact protection ([Fig. 4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264127522006165#f0020)(c)). To ensure low weight while optimizing [rigidity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/rigidity "Learn more about rigidity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [Mercedes Benz](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/mercedes-benz "Learn more about Mercedes Benz from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") employed intelligent material composition with high aluminum content to manufacture the bodyshell architecture of the new AMG SL ([Fig. 4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264127522006165#f0020)(d)). Despite the weight reduction potential and other promising benefits, there are a series of critical factors to be considered before the massive production of aluminum-based automobiles, primarily including technical (_e.g_., the aluminum component performance, design flexibility, manufacturability, crashworthiness, and maintenance) and economic concerns (_e.g_., the initial costs of raw materials and expenses during manufacturing or operating processes), as well as the environmental impacts (_e.g_., the post-consumer recycling potential and disposal of scraps).

![Image 15](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0264127522006165-gr4.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (783KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0264127522006165-gr4_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (783KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0264127522006165-gr4.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 4. (a) Yearly change of [aluminum](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/aluminum "Learn more about aluminum from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") content in the light vehicle from 1975 to 2030 (North American market), data from Light Metal Age; (b) Predicted proportion of different types of [aluminum alloy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/aluminum-alloys "Learn more about aluminum alloy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in 2030; (c) Honda Pilot frontal crash test (from the Insurance Institute for Highway Safety (IIHS)), where an extruded aluminum bumper beam plays a significant role in impact protection (enlarged area); (d) New Mercedes AMG SL with high aluminum content in the bodyshell architecture to lower weight while achieving maximum [rigidity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/rigidity "Learn more about rigidity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (from Mercedes Benz); (e) Illustration of recycling aluminum systems in passenger vehicles [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264127522006165#b0135).

[Aluminum alloys](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/aluminum-alloys "Learn more about Aluminum alloys from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are amenable to different manufacturing processes, including both wrought and cast compositions, where wrought aluminum alloys (mainly produced from the primary aluminum) usually contain relatively lower alloy content in contrast to that of the [cast alloys](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/cast-alloy "Learn more about cast alloys from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[[23]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264127522006165#b0115). The wrought aluminum alloys are available in different forms (e.g., sheet, extrusion, and forging), while the casting ones enable rather complicated shapes (e.g., aluminum [powertrain](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/powertrain "Learn more about powertrain from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") components) by extensive [casting processes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/casting-process "Learn more about casting processes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (e.g., [sand casting](h

enriched_points.expanded_content: Utilizing renewable resources like hemp and flax, bio-based materials provide a lightweight, sustainable alternative to petroleum-based plastics, enhancing automotive design while meeting environmental goals.
Smart materials adapt to environmental changes, offering enhanced performance features such as self-healing and temperature regulation, which optimize vehicle efficiency and safety in real-time.

Innovative methods like 3D printing and automated assembly reduce production costs and material waste, enabling the creation of complex, lightweight components tailored for modern automotive applications.
